title: Bracket Uniqueness and Generation Strategy: Enumeration vs Sampling

per_region.explanation: Each region is a 16-team single-elimination bracket with 15 games. Each game has exactly 2 outcomes (win/lose), so there are 2^15 = 32,768 possible region brackets. This is an exact, exhaustive count — no approximation needed.
per_region.encoding: Each region bracket maps to a 15-bit integer (0 to 32767). Bit i represents the outcome of game i. This is a perfect bijection: every integer in [0, 32767] is a valid region bracket, and every region bracket maps to exactly one integer.

full_tournament.breakdown: 4 regions x 15 games = 60 region games + 3 Final Four games = 63 total
full_tournament.cross_region_combinations: 32,768^4 = 1,152,921,504,606,846,976 (1.15 quintillion) region combinations, times 2^3 = 8 Final Four outcomes = 9.2 quintillion total
full_tournament.explanation: Full-bracket enumeration is impossible. Even at 1 billion brackets/second, it would take 292 years.

plausible_brackets.definition: A bracket where P(bracket) > some threshold epsilon
plausible_brackets.analysis: With typical win probabilities (1-seed vs 16-seed ~98.7%), the vast majority of 32,768 region brackets have negligible probability. A 16-seed-wins-all bracket has probability ~10^-30. However, ALL 32,768 are valid and computable — the question is which ones matter.

effective_count_per_region.above_1e_minus_10: roughly 5,000-10,000 brackets depending on probability model
effective_count_per_region.note: These are estimates. The exact counts depend on the pairwise win probability matrix.

the_uniqueness_insight.key_realization: With only 32,768 possible region brackets, the original plan to generate 3M per region is fundamentally misguided. You CANNOT have 3M unique region brackets — only 32,768 exist. The 3M plan implicitly creates ~91 copies of each bracket on average.
the_uniqueness_insight.what_3m_actually_means: Under Monte Carlo sampling, you sample 3M brackets from the probability distribution. High-probability brackets get sampled many times (thousands of copies), low-probability brackets may never appear. The 3M samples are just a noisy approximation of the exact probability distribution over 32,768 outcomes.
the_uniqueness_insight.the_better_approach: Since 32,768 is trivially enumerable, we can compute EXACT probabilities for every region bracket. No sampling noise. No duplicates. Perfect coverage. This is orders of magnitude more efficient.

approach_1_naive_monte_carlo.name: Naive Monte Carlo (3M samples per region)
approach_1_naive_monte_carlo.description: Simulate 3M brackets per region by sampling each game outcome according to win probability. Store all 12M brackets in PostgreSQL.
pros: Natural uncertainty quantification via sample variance
cons: Massive redundancy: 3M samples across 32,768 possible outcomes = ~91x duplication on average
Sampling noise: rare but interesting brackets (e.g., 11-seed champion) may be underrepresented
Wastes storage: 12M rows in PostgreSQL when 131,072 (32K x 4 regions) suffice per region
Wastes compute: generating and inserting millions of duplicate brackets
approach_1_naive_monte_carlo.verdict: REJECTED — unnecessary given enumeration is feasible

approach_2_stratified_importance_samplin.name: Stratified Importance Sampling (original architecture plan)
approach_2_stratified_importance_samplin.description: Define strata by (R1 upset count, R2 upset count, champion seed). Allocate 3M budget per region using Neyman allocation (proportional to sqrt(P(stratum))). Assign weights to each sampled bracket.
pros: Better coverage of rare events than naive MC
cons: Still sampling from a space of only 32,768 outcomes — unnecessary complexity
Strata definition adds implementation burden for no real gain when enumeration is possible
Weights add complexity to all downstream queries
approach_2_stratified_importance_samplin.verdict: SUPERSEDED — enumeration eliminates the need for importance sampling at the region level

approach_3_full_region_enumeration.name: Full Region Enumeration + Cross-Region Sampling (RECOMMENDED)
approach_3_full_region_enumeration.description: Enumerate all 32,768 region brackets for each of 4 regions. Compute exact probability for each. For full-bracket analysis, either (a) combine lazily on demand, or (b) sample cross-region combinations weighted by joint probability.
pros: EXACT probabilities — zero sampling error for per-region statistics
Complete coverage — every possible outcome is represented, including rare upsets
Tiny storage footprint: 131,072 rows total (32K x 4 regions)
Trivial to compute: NumPy generates all 32,768 in microseconds
Bayesian updating is exact: just re-weight and renormalize
Pruning is simple: set eliminated brackets' weight to 0, renormalize
No strata, no sampling weights, no Neyman allocation — simpler code
cons: Cross-region combinations (32,768^4 ~ 10^18) cannot be fully enumerated
Full-bracket statistics (e.g., national champion probability) require either (a) conditional probability formula or (b) sampling from cross-region joint distribution
Loses the 'portfolio of specific full brackets' metaphor
approach_3_full_region_enumeration.verdict: RECOMMENDED — best balance of exactness, simplicity, and efficiency

approach_4_exact_probability_computation.name: Exact Probability via Phylogenetic/Recursive Algorithm (No Storage)
approach_4_exact_probability_computation.description: Use the Felsenstein pruning algorithm (adapted for tournaments) to compute exact per-team advancement probabilities without enumerating any brackets. As described in the Phylourny paper (2023) and Brandes et al. 'Stop Simulating!' (2023/2025).
pros: Fastest computation: equivalent to ~100-200 MC simulations but gives exact results
No storage needed for bracket database
Exact probabilities for any team reaching any round
cons: Only computes per-team advancement probabilities, not full bracket distributions
Cannot maintain a 'portfolio of brackets' for live pruning/tracking
Does not support the tracker use case (pruning live brackets as results come in)
Academic papers; less battle-tested for production bracket pools
approach_4_exact_probability_computation.verdict: USE FOR VALIDATION — compute exact advancement probabilities to validate our enumerated bracket weights, but not as primary architecture

recommended_architecture.name: Hybrid: Region Enumeration + Cross-Region Sampling + Exact Validation
recommended_architecture.summary: Enumerate all 32,768 region brackets per region (131,072 total), compute exact probabilities. For the live tracker, sample a manageable number of full brackets (cross-region combinations) for the pruning use case. Validate with exact recursive algorithm.

layer_1_region_enumeration.description: Enumerate all 2^15 = 32,768 region brackets for each of 4 regions

implementation.step_1: Generate all 32,768 binary vectors: np.arange(32768, dtype=np.uint16)
implementation.step_2: Unpack bits to get game outcomes: ((ids[:, None] >> np.arange(15)) & 1).astype(np.int8)
implementation.step_3: For each bracket, simulate the region: determine which teams advance based on game outcomes
implementation.step_4: Compute P(bracket) = product of P(game_outcome | teams_that_reached_that_game)
implementation.step_5: Validate: sum of all 32,768 probabilities must equal 1.0 (within floating point tolerance)
implementation.step_6: Store: bracket_id (SMALLINT), packed_result (SMALLINT), probability (FLOAT4), champion_seed (SMALLINT), champion_team_id (INT), round_results (JSONB optional)

storage.comparison: vs 320 MB for 12M sampled brackets = 160x reduction

exact_statistics_available: P(team X wins region) — exact, by summing probabilities of brackets where X is champion
P(team X reaches round R) — exact, by summing probabilities of brackets where X reaches round R
P(seed S wins region) — exact, by summing probabilities of brackets where seed S is champion
P(exactly k upsets in round R) — exact, by summing matching brackets
Expected number of upsets per round — exact
Full probability distribution of any region-level statistic — exact

layer_2_cross_region_full_brackets.description: For the live tracker use case, we need actual full brackets to prune. Sample full brackets by combining region brackets weighted by joint probability.

option_a_lazy_combination.description: Do NOT pre-generate full brackets. When a game result comes in, prune the affected region's brackets (set weight=0, renormalize). Cross-region statistics computed on-the-fly by multiplying independent region probabilities.
option_a_lazy_combination.pros: Minimal storage, exact math, instant pruning
option_a_lazy_combination.cons: Cannot display 'number of surviving brackets' as a single integer (it's a product of surviving counts per region)
option_a_lazy_combination.suitability: Best if the tracker only needs per-region and per-team statistics

option_b_sampled_full_brackets.description: Pre-sample N full brackets (e.g., 1M-3M) by independently sampling one region bracket per region, weighted by probability. Store as 4 SMALLINT columns (one per region) + weight.
option_b_sampled_full_brackets.how_to_sample: For each full bracket: sample south_bracket_id from Categorical(P_south), east_bracket_id from Categorical(P_east), etc. P(full_bracket) = P(south) * P(east) * P(west) * P(midwest) since regions are independent until Final Four.
option_b_sampled_full_brackets.final_four_handling: After selecting 4 region champions, simulate 3 Final Four games. This adds 2^3 = 8 possible outcomes per 4-region combination. Can enumerate all 8 or sample.
option_b_sampled_full_brackets.pros: Provides concrete full brackets for display, counting survivors is intuitive
option_b_sampled_full_brackets.cons: Returns to sampling (though from exact region distributions), storage grows
option_b_sampled_full_brackets.suitability: Best if the tracker UI shows 'X brackets still alive' as a headline number

option_c_top_k_per_region.description: Keep only the top-K most probable region brackets (e.g., K=1000) per region. Full bracket space = 1000^4 = 10^12 — still too large to enumerate, but region pruning is faster.
option_c_top_k_per_region.pros: Reduces noise from astronomically unlikely brackets
option_c_top_k_per_region.cons: Loses coverage of rare upsets, introduces selection bias
option_c_top_k_per_region.suitability: Not recommended — loses the exact enumeration advantage

layer_2_cross_region_full_brackets.recommendation: option_a_lazy_combination for most use cases. option_b with 1M-3M sampled full brackets if the UI requires a concrete bracket count.

layer_3_exact_validation.description: Use the Phylourny / Felsenstein pruning algorithm to compute exact per-team advancement probabilities. Compare against our enumerated probabilities as a validation step.
layer_3_exact_validation.implementation: The recursive algorithm computes P(team_i wins tournament) in O(n^2) time. For 16 teams per region: 256 operations per team, 4096 total per region. Trivial.
layer_3_exact_validation.purpose: Catches bugs in our enumeration logic. If our summed probabilities disagree with the recursive algorithm, we have an implementation error.

region_enumeration: def enumerate_region_brackets(teams: list[Team], win_prob_matrix: np.ndarray) -> RegionBrackets:
# teams: 16 teams in seed order (1-seed at index 0, 16-seed at index 15)
# win_prob_matrix: 16x16 matrix where [i][j] = P(team i beats team j)
# Unpack all 15 game outcomes for all 32768 brackets at once
# Shape: (32768, 15) — each row is one bracket, each column is one game outcome
# For each bracket, determine which teams play and win each game
# This requires knowing the bracket structure (which teams meet in which game)
probabilities = np.ones(n_brackets, dtype=np.float64)  # use float64 for products
# Process all brackets in parallel using vectorized operations
# Game 0: seed 1 vs seed 16, Game 1: seed 8 vs seed 9, ...
outcome = game_outcomes[:, game_idx]  # 0 = team_a wins, 1 = team_b wins
# Round 2: 4 games (game indices 8-11), winners of adjacent R1 games meet
team_a = round1_winners[:, 2*game_idx]      # winner of R1 game 2k
team_b = round1_winners[:, 2*game_idx + 1]   # winner of R1 game 2k+1
# Vectorized probability lookup: P(team_a[i] beats team_b[i]) for each bracket i
assert abs(total - 1.0) < 1e-10, f'Probabilities sum to {total}, expected 1.0'
packed_results=bracket_ids,  # the ID IS the packed result
implementation_pseudocode.probability_computation_note: The probability of a bracket is the product of probabilities of each game outcome. For game g with outcome o, if o=0 (higher seed wins), multiply by P(A beats B). If o=1 (lower seed wins), multiply by P(B beats A) = 1 - P(A beats B). The win probability P(A beats B) depends on WHICH teams reach game g, which depends on outcomes of earlier games — this is why we must process rounds sequentially, but within each round we can vectorize across all 32,768 brackets.

region_enumeration_in_memory.bracket_ids: 32768 x uint16 = 64 KB per region, 256 KB total
region_enumeration_in_memory.game_outcomes_unpacked: 32768 x 15 x int8 = 480 KB per region, 1.92 MB total (temporary, for computation)
region_enumeration_in_memory.probabilities: 32768 x float32 = 128 KB per region, 512 KB total
region_enumeration_in_memory.champions: 32768 x int16 = 64 KB per region, 256 KB total
region_enumeration_in_memory.total_in_memory: ~3 MB for all 4 regions (vs ~500 MB for 12M sampled brackets)

postgresql_storage.table_schema: region_brackets(region VARCHAR(10), bracket_id SMALLINT, packed_result SMALLINT, probability FLOAT4, champion_seed SMALLINT, champion_team_id INT, is_alive BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE)

if_1m_full_brackets.schema: full_brackets(id SERIAL, south_bracket_id SMALLINT, east_bracket_id SMALLINT, west_bracket_id SMALLINT, midwest_bracket_id SMALLINT, final_four_result SMALLINT, weight FLOAT4, is_alive BOOLEAN)

how_this_changes_the_original_plan.original_plan: 3M brackets per region, 12M total, stored in PostgreSQL partitioned by region. Stratified importance sampling with Neyman allocation. Brackets have weights for Bayesian updating.
how_this_changes_the_original_plan.revised_plan: 32,768 brackets per region, 131,072 total. Full enumeration with exact probabilities. Optionally sample 1M-3M full brackets for the live tracker UI. No stratification needed.

changes.impact: 91x reduction in rows, ZERO sampling error
No Monte Carlo noise, perfect accuracy

changes.old: Minutes to generate 12M brackets + COPY insert
UPDATE brackets SET is_alive=FALSE WHERE bitwise condition
changes.new: Same SQL, but over 32K rows instead of 3M — instant
changes.impact: Pruning queries go from seconds to milliseconds

changes.new: Set eliminated brackets to weight=0, renormalize remaining — exact posterior
Compute exact P(1-seed wins region) by summing weights — no threshold check needed
changes.impact: Validation is a simple assertion on exact probability

what_stays_the_same: 15-bit encoding per region bracket (SMALLINT) — this is perfect, keeps it
Win probability blend (40% stats + 45% market + 15% factors) — still determines P(A beats B)
Power index formula (9 factors) — still used to compute P_stats
PostgreSQL storage with partitioning by region — still useful, just fewer rows
Live tracker pruning via bitwise SQL — same logic, faster execution
Weight-based analysis — probabilities ARE the weights

final_four_handling.problem: Regions are independent until the Final Four. Once we have 4 region champions, they play 3 more games (2 semifinals + 1 final). How do we handle this?

solution.description: For each combination of 4 region champions, enumerate all 2^3 = 8 Final Four outcomes. Compute P(Final Four outcome) = product of 3 game probabilities. Total full-bracket probability = P(south_region) * P(east_region) * P(west_region) * P(midwest_region) * P(final_four_outcome).
solution.plausible_champion_combinations: In practice, ~500-2000 tuples cover >99.9% of probability mass
solution.total_full_bracket_specifications: 65,536 champion tuples * 8 FF outcomes = 524,288 (or ~8,000-16,000 for plausible only)
solution.exact_national_champion_probability: Sum over all region brackets where team X is region champion, multiply by P(team X wins Final Four given opponents). This is exact and fast.

stop_simulating.title: Stop Simulating! Efficient Computation of Tournament Winning Probabilities
stop_simulating.authors: Ulrik Brandes, Gordana Marmulla, Ivana Smokovic
stop_simulating.key_finding: Exact computation of tournament winning probabilities can be substantially faster than Monte Carlo approximation. The algorithm computes exact probabilities in time equivalent to a few hundred simulation runs.
stop_simulating.relevance: Validates our enumeration approach. If exact computation is faster than simulation for FIFA World Cup (32 teams), it is trivially fast for 16-team regions.

phylourny.title: Phylourny: efficiently calculating elimination tournament win probabilities via phylogenetic methods
phylourny.key_finding: Uses Felsenstein pruning algorithm (from computational phylogenetics) to compute exact per-team tournament win probabilities. O(n^2) complexity. Two orders of magnitude faster than simulation.
phylourny.relevance: Provides a validation algorithm for our enumerated probabilities. Can also compute P(team reaches round R) exactly.

fivethirtyeight_method.title: How Our March Madness Predictions Work
fivethirtyeight_method.key_finding: FiveThirtyEight does NOT use Monte Carlo for March Madness. They compute conditional probabilities directly: P(team reaches round R) = P(team reaches round R-1) * sum over possible opponents of [P(beat opponent) * P(opponent reaches round R-1)]. This is the recursive exact computation approach.
fivethirtyeight_method.relevance: Industry validation that exact computation is superior to simulation for single-elimination tournaments.

rationale: 32,768 possible region brackets is trivially enumerable — there is no reason to sample from this space
Enumeration gives EXACT probabilities with ZERO sampling error
Storage drops from ~400 MB to ~7 MB (57x reduction)
Computation drops from minutes to seconds
Pruning queries drop from seconds to milliseconds
Code complexity drops: eliminate stratifier.py, simplify engine.py
FiveThirtyEight and academic researchers (Phylourny, 'Stop Simulating!') all converge on exact computation over simulation for single-elimination tournaments
The only place sampling is still needed is for cross-region full brackets (for the live tracker UI), and even there 1M samples is more than sufficient
action_items: 1. Refactor simulation/engine.py to enumerate all 32,768 region brackets instead of sampling 3M
2. Remove simulation/stratifier.py (stratification is unnecessary with enumeration)
4. Add validation: assert sum(probabilities) == 1.0 for each region
5. Add Phylourny-style exact validation as a cross-check
6. Optionally sample 1M-3M full brackets for live tracker UI (cross-region combinations)
7. Update CLAUDE.md to reflect the new architecture
8. Update database schema: region_brackets table is tiny, remove partitioning (unnecessary for 32K rows per region)
9. Update pruning logic: same bitwise approach but over 32K rows = instant
